See `./py3status/tests/test_formatter.py` for various examples.

We make adjustment in `xrandr` code to pass out number of outputs as `{count}` or `{output_num}` placeholder. None of that hide options. Simple raw code.

If you don't want single combination, we use the formatter to tweak things away to our desires... I imagine it would be something like this.
`format = [\?more_than_one=count {output}]` _Maybe bad name. not actual example_.

We already can do this kind of this thing now if we keep getting more formatter options in the future, it's totally doable. Also, this is nice because it would just work with all modules instead.

I'm assuming we want to deprecate `fixed_width` in this code eventually once we get a working formatter for `fixed_width` so we just use `[\?fixed_width {output}]` instead.

IDEAL GOAL: 
```
count = len(self.available_combinations)
self.py3.safe_format(self.format, {'output': output, 'count': count})
```
```
format = [\?more_than_one=count {output}]
```
The `[{output}]` thing is already in the formatter to hide values if we don't get any. No need to worry about that. Really, this is confusing for me too because I don't know what quite works well. There are more wish features in here though. https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/issues/752 😄

The `format_` prefix (in the config) denotes that we can use the formatting tricks here... and `format_` prefix (in the placeholder) denotes that it'll be something formatted.

We often use `format_` prefix (in the placeholder) when there can be more than one element... and it is usually done in the loop like the events here... because sometimes we can have more placeholders to choose from... and/or sometimes the placeholders can give us different values too... Doing this way will give users more freedom to customize... as we can let the users decide what to hide... or what to show... using basic formatting tricks.

Let me show you the future. Look at my recent `keyboard_locks` pull request where I'm getting rid of the icons in favor of putting everything in the format...

Now, let me show you the past. Look at current `dpms`. We have `icon_off`, `icon_on` there. Those config params used to be known as `format_off`, `format_on`... which is a bit confusing because we have `format` everywhere now... due to this pull request https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/issues/98. Lot of things to catch up. 👍 

Future config...
```
dpms {
    # full customizability, no hardcoded.
    # can change all colors, texts, and format here.
    format = '\?if=is_enabled ON|OFF'
}
```
Current config...  Pull request to future: `404 Not Found` :-(
```
dpms {
    # full customizability, no hardcoded, lot of silly configs.
    # lot of configs to change all colors, texts, and format.
    format = `{icon}`
    icon_off = 'OFF'
    icon_on = 'ON'
}
```
Past config... Pull request to the current: https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/pull/640
```
dpms {
    # partial customizability for text and colors, but not format.
    # format does not exist at this point. hardcoded.
    # fomat can't deal with prefix or suffix, only configs.
    format_on = `ON`
    format_off = `OFF`
}
```
This does not exist, but if it does... It wouldn't make sense.
```
dpms {
    #  same as above, but can cause confusion. we use icon.
    format = '\?if=dpms {format_on}|{format_off}'
    format_on = `ON`
    format_off = `OFF`
}
```
This may be somewhat to what you're doing here.
```
dpms {
    # partial customizability, where is '{on}' coming from? hardcoded
    format = '\?if=dpms {on}'
    format_on = `ON`
    format_off = `OFF`
}
```
I hope that helps a lot. :-)

We've been debating about whether we should put this kind of functionality directly into the `Tokenizer`, as it's a common problem to have.  What do you think?

Some issues:
- Wanting to discard instances completely if they exceed some max length seems like it's functionality that belongs in the `DatasetReader`, not the `Tokenizer` (the tokenizer could return `None` here in those cases, but that seems awkward).  And if you have one `Tokenizer` that operates on both questions and passages in SQuAD, you might want different max lengths for each of those, so you'd want the `DatasetReader` to pass these lengths to `tokenize()`, not have them be parameter that get passed to the `Tokenizer's` constructor.
- On the other hand, `Tokenizers` can add start and end tokens to their strings (which are separate from the `START_SYMBOL` and `END_SYMBOL` here - these are required for decoder functionality, but you might want additional buffer tokens if you're using a CNN to encode things).  So the truncation that you're doing outside of the `Tokenizer` messes with any start and end tokens that the tokenizer may have added.

Any ideas?

I tried my hand at this but ran into some issues. I'll keep playing with it at some point, but I'll post what I've come across already here:

We definitely need to read the file as bytes:

    contents = io.open(filename, 'rb').read()

That breaks casting to a list, but fortunately there's a byte-equivalent for that

    splitcontents = bytearray(contents)

That also means we need to tokenize differently; fortunately, `tokenize.tokenize` handles bytes:

    tokens = reversed(list(tokenize.tokenize(io.BytesIO(contents).readline, )))

When setting the new characters in the loop, we need to modify a `bytearray` now instead of a `list`. That'll be _something along the lines of_:

    splitcontents[...] = int.from_bytes(new_text.encode(), byteorder='big')

Finally, we can set

    new_contents = b''.join(splitcontents)

The above runs into a problem where somewhere along the lines I f*cked up and started tracking characters instead of lines (or something like that? In progress, still needs work, if anyone has any interest in carrying on with this _be my guest_).

`reader.file` is like the return value of `file = open(file, "rb")` if opened in `TTFile.__init__` i.e.:

```py
>>> file = open('../fonts/apache/roboto/Roboto-Regular.ttf', 'rb')
>>> file.name
'../fonts/apache/roboto/Roboto-Regular.ttf'
```

But an edge case here is that a file-like object can not have a name attribute, e.g. a [io.BytesIO](https://docs.python.org/2/library/io.html#io.BytesIO):

```py
>>> file1 = open('../fonts/apache/roboto/Roboto-Regular.ttf', 'rb')
>>> file1.name
'../fonts/apache/roboto/Roboto-Regular.ttf'
>>> from io import BytesIO
>>> file2 = BytesIO(file1.read())
# no name!
>>> file2.name
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

> By the same logic why not just make the whole file 1 test surely?

Well indeed. An integration test makes sense a single workflow from setup to testing to teardown. Of course there has to be a balance between putting out entire test suite in one test function and putting each `assert` in its own function.

However, The reason to split up your tests into separate functions is so that when you see a single test failing you will be able to do:
```bash
py.test python/Ganga/test/Unit/LCG/TestGrid.py::test_submit_expired_proxy
```
without having to run *all* the LCG tests.

In our case here, doing
```bash
py.test python/Ganga/test/GPI/FileTests/TestLocalFileClient.py::test_b_testClientSideComplete
```
will fail since the environment has not been set up. The whole point of a framework like Python's `unittest` is to make sure that you can run your test functions and have them *always* set up correctly.
